metadata.title: Cross-National News Narratives of the Paralympic Games: Computational Text Analysis of the Media Coverage in the United States and South Korea

abstract: This study presents a comparison of themes found in the U.S. and South Korean news coverage of the Paralympics. It is commonplace to associate rugged individualism with the former country and collectivism with the latter, and we assumed that these national traits would be apparent in the news coverage of the Paralympic Games in the two countries. Using the keyword "Paralympics," we searched two decades of newspaper coverage and performed latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling on the articles that the search produced. The results of the modeling confirmed that the reporting in each country reflected its national characteristics, with the U.S. coverage reinforcing ableism and the South Korean coverage focusing on the government's efforts to promote large-scale sporting events. The news stories in both countries were alike, though, in treating disabled athletes and traditional athletes differently. Part of the contribution of this research to the academic study of disabled athletes is the use of computational textual analysis to address these issues.

paragraphs: The Paralympic Games have contributed to an increase in the public awareness of individuals and athletes with disabilities by promoting disability equality, diversity, and inclusion (Pullen et al., 2019). However, disability sports are rarely visible in the mass media (Hardin & Hardin, 2003). As DePauw (1997) and Buysse and Borcherding (2010) pointed out, the participants seem not to meet the socially constructed ideal of physical ability on which people without disabilities agree.

Nevertheless, the largest number of countries participated in the 2018 Paralympic Games in Pyeongchang, South Korea, and the increase in the interest in the games among audiences and sports journalists worldwide suggests that there is a market for media coverage of such events (Kolotouchkina et al., 2021). The mass media and sports have long enjoyed a symbiotic relationship (McChesney, 1989) and exerted a powerful influence on society (Pedersen et al., 2003). Accordingly, media representations of para-sports influence perceptions of athletes with disabilities (McPherson et al., 2016). Further, the visibility of athletes with disabilities in the coverage of the Paralympic Games by mainstream media outlets can facilitate efforts to create a more equitable world for individuals with disabilities, athletes and otherwise (Pullen et al., 2020: Silva & Howe, 2012). The coverage of the event has increased since the 2000 Sydney Paralympic Games when athletes with disabilities started to use advanced equipment and garner considerable attention from reporters (Howe, 2008;Schantz & Gilbert, 2001). In South Korea, news reports described the Paralympic games as something of a sideshow, but the media perspective has been evolving toward greater coverage of the event since 2000 (Woo, 2007).

Previous studies of the media coverage of the Paralympic Games and athletes with disabilities have focused on stereotypes such as the "supercrip" narrative that emphasizes the challenges overcome by people with disabilities in daily life (Kama, 2004) and have suffered from ableism, that is, an ability-based perspective on the value of individuals (Wolbring, 2011). Also, there have been few cross-national comparisons of the media coverage of the games (e.g., Baek, 2012;Buysse & Borcherding, 2010;Schantz & Gilbert, 2001) or studies of the issue that have made use of computational big data analysis. Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling based on the entire news coverage of the Paralympic Games in the United States and South Korea can help fill this gap. These countries provide an instructive comparison because the culture in the former has a more individualist orientation (Barr, 2000;Butterworth, 2007) and that in the latter a more collectivist orientation (Hofstede, 2001;Lee, 2001). These national characteristics-the "rugged individualism" of the United States (Hardin & Hardin, 2003) and collectivism of South Korea (Lee et al., 2018)-are reflected in the news coverage by media outlets in the two countries (Gans, 1979). We reasoned that this trend would be observable in word usage in newspaper articles over decades and could be detected through big data analysis that can provide nuanced perspectives on the issues and topics written about in newspapers (Rodriguez, 2017).

The purpose of this study was to test the assumption that the news coverage of the Paralympics reflects the distinctive cultural backgrounds of the United States and South Korea. Our method involved comparing all of the coverage of the Paralympic Games by major national newspapers in the U.S. and South Korea over the past two decades by means of LDA topic modeling. Because this type of modeling helps researchers to identify thematic structures within a large volume of documents, we considered it suitable for observing thematic differences in the coverage in the two countries. The results of the modeling informed our qualitative interpretations of the coverage and establishment of the textual patterns for each topic. This study expands the theoretical basis for exploring cross-cultural phenomena such as the increased news coverage of the Paralympic Games. In practical terms, the findings presented here can inform the drafting of ethical guidelines for the Paralympic Games that reflect the perspective of athletes with disabilities.

sections.header_raw: Cultural Backgrounds on Media Descriptions of the Paralympic Games
sections.header_norm: cultural backgrounds on media descriptions of the paralympic games
paragraphs: Scholars have observed that media outlets frame news stories in ways that reflect the prevailing social values and political ideologies (Kwon & Moon, 2009). Gans (1979) spoke of nations' "enduring values" (p. 42) that inform, often unconsciously, reporters' presentation of news stories. The enduring values apparent in the coverage by U.S. media outlets are often described by the catch-phrase "rugged individualism" (Hardin & Hardin, 2003), while those apparent in the South Korean media coverage are often described as "collectivist" (Lee et al., 2018).

Rugged individualism refers to the notions that, ideally, citizens remain self-reliant, rejecting most assistance from any level of government, and that one's own goals are more important than the goals of others (Triandis, 2018). Thus, cross-cultural studies (Hsu, 1983;Triandis, 2018) have suggested that individualistic cultures highlight selfsufficiency, personal efficacy, and the necessity of personal struggle and achievement. This personal focus is consistent with the application of the supercrip theme to athletes with disabilities, whose individual abilities and achievements are depicted as overcoming their disadvantages. U.S. sports reporting often reinforces rugged individualism, and devalues interdependence as weak and undesirable behavior (Barr, 2000).

South Korean culture, by contrast, has consistently been described as collectivist, meaning that the focus in society tends to be on community, consensus, and harmony (Hofstede, 2001). Such a collectivist self-image, or "we-ness," emphasizes shared community goals and accommodating others' needs and wants (Melucci, 1995). In this regard, collectivist societies often ask individuals to suppress the self in the interest of the broader society (Tan, 2012). South Korean culture, in sum, emphasizes unity and the public good over the achievement of individual goals (Lee, 2001). Asian journalism in general emphasizes communalism and consensus, with media outlets advocating in their coverage for the subordination of individual needs to the goals of the community or nation to maintain social stability (Massey & Chang, 2002).

Naturally, media outlets play a central role in disseminating messaging associated with significant events. The argument here is that, in the case of the Paralympic Games, while the U.S. media coverage has been using frames such as the supercrip narrative that focus on individual athletes with disabilities (Hardin & Hardin, 2003) in ways that reflect U.S. culture in general, the South Korean coverage has been more collectivist in its orientation in keeping with that country's culture (Ayhan, 2017).

sections.header_raw: The Supercrip Narrative and Ableism on the Individual Level
sections.header_norm: the supercrip narrative and ableism on the individual level
paragraphs: The narratives about athletes and others with disabilities have included, along with the supercrip narrative (Hargreaves & Hardin, 2009), the winning of medals for one's nation (Chang & Crossman, 2009;Solves et al., 2018), able-nationalism (Pullen et al., 2019(Pullen et al., , 2020)), and the differences between domestic and international athletes (Bruce, 2014). The supercrip narrative has been the most common of these narratives in the news coverage of athletes with disabilities and the Paralympic Games (Hargreaves & Hardin, 2009;Hodges et al., 2015;Silva & Howe, 2012). Berger (2008) described supercrips as "those individuals whose inspirational stories of courage, dedication, and hard work prove that it can be done, that one can defy the odds and accomplish the impossible" (p. 648). Hardin andHardin (2003, 2004) argued that the supercrip stereotype is ubiquitous in the media coverage of disability sports and has framed disabled people as deserving pity unless they overcome their disabilities through personal effort. The mainstream media outlets, accordingly, cover the achievements of disabled individuals as heroic feats (Schell & Rodriguez, 2001). The basic premise of the supercrip narrative rests on the assumption that disability is an individual condition that the broader society rarely has the responsibility to accommodate (Hardin & Hardin, 2003). Silva and Howe (2012; see also Howe & Silva, 2017) argued that the supercrip narrative can be positive when it is uncritically interpreted but that it tends to lower expectations regarding disabled individuals so that any achievement, however meaningful, seems worthy of praise easily. Haller (1999) argued that individuals with disabilities tend to reject this stereotype out of concern that those without disabilities may attribute any lower performance on their part to a lack of will or determination. Thus, while the supercrip narrative emphasizes the possibility of overcoming disability through hard work, it underestimates the attitudinal barriers that individuals with disabilities face (Ellis & Goggin, 2015). Specifically, the supercrip narrative tends to ignore the complexity of the experiences of people with disabilities in favor of a view that disability is a concern for individuals rather than for the broader society (Silva & Howe, 2012). For these reasons, Stella Young, a disability rights activist, coined the term "inspirational porn" to describe the supercrip narrative and criticize the conception that people with disabilities are reducible to inspirational stories (Haller & Preston, 2016).

Regarding the tendency of the supercrip narrative to perpetuate ableism (Kearney et al., 2019), it is useful to view ableism in the context of the account of media coverage of athletes with disabilities offered by critical disability studies (CDS; Campbell, 2009;Goodley & Lawthom, 2019). From the perspective of CDS, disabled individuals suffer from social oppression and can only increase their social capital through selective negotiation based on the narratives of those without disabilities, which emphasize the dominance and superiority of the latter over the former (Bottema-Beutel et al., 2021;Goffman, 2009). CDS conceptualizes ableism as the belief that disabilities are inherently negative and should be corrected when possible (Campbell, 2009). As an othering mechanism, ableism reflects the fact that societies have nearly always been designed for and by individuals without disabilities (Duncan, 2001) and the attitude that equates able-bodiedness with normalcy and disability with abnormality (Ho, 2008). In this respect, the language of disability discourse is inherently political, serving as it does to construct social attitudes (Oliver & Barnes, 2010). Therefore, some progressive media scholars have called for moving beyond the stereotypical narratives to provide more equitable coverage of athletes with disabilities (e.g., Hardin & Hardin, 2004). The literature has established conclusively that media descriptions have contributed to the stereotypical views of athletes with disabilities.

The close association between the supercrip narrative and the theme of individualism as it is articulated in U.S. culture is attributable to the fact that U.S. sports media have tended to valorize neoliberal ideals such as rugged individualism and hard work for the purpose of remasculinizing the culture (Kusz, 2007), especially in the wake of 9/ 11 (Falcous & Silk, 2006). The heroic conception of individual athletes reinforces the emphasis on strength, power, and male dominance associated with rugged individualism (Butterworth, 2007). Thus, the supercrip narrative, originating in and perpetuating ableism, emphasizes individual efforts to overcome disabilities and display ablebodiedness. Studies of the news coverage of the Paralympic Games have, naturally, identified the supercrip narrative (Brooke, 2019;McGillivray et al., 2019;Souza & Brittain, 2020). Pullen et al. (2020) argued that "able-nationalism" in sports emphasizes the inclusion of certain disabilities and the othering of individuals with disabilities who do not display exceptional performance.

Silva and Howe (2012; see also Howe & Silva, 2017) argued that the supercrip narrative can be positive when it is uncritically interpreted but that it tends to lower expectations regarding disabled individuals so that any achievement, however meaningful, seems worthy of praise easily. Haller (1999) argued that individuals with disabilities tend to reject this stereotype out of concern that those without disabilities may attribute any lower performance on their part to a lack of will or determination. Thus, while the supercrip narrative emphasizes the possibility of overcoming disability through hard work, it underestimates the attitudinal barriers that individuals with disabilities face (Ellis & Goggin, 2015). Specifically, the supercrip narrative tends to ignore the complexity of the experiences of people with disabilities in favor of a view that disability is a concern for individuals rather than for the broader society (Silva & Howe, 2012). For these reasons, Stella Young, a disability rights activist, coined the term "inspirational porn" to describe the supercrip narrative and criticize the conception that people with disabilities are reducible to inspirational stories (Haller & Preston, 2016).

Regarding the tendency of the supercrip narrative to perpetuate ableism (Kearney et al., 2019), it is useful to view ableism in the context of the account of media coverage of athletes with disabilities offered by critical disability studies (CDS; Campbell, 2009;Goodley & Lawthom, 2019). From the perspective of CDS, disabled individuals suffer from social oppression and can only increase their social capital through selective negotiation based on the narratives of those without disabilities, which emphasize the dominance and superiority of the latter over the former (Bottema-Beutel et al., 2021;Goffman, 2009). CDS conceptualizes ableism as the belief that disabilities are inherently negative and should be corrected when possible (Campbell, 2009). As an othering mechanism, ableism reflects the fact that societies have nearly always been designed for and by individuals without disabilities (Duncan, 2001) and the attitude that equates able-bodiedness with normalcy and disability with abnormality (Ho, 2008). In this respect, the language of disability discourse is inherently political, serving as it does to construct social attitudes (Oliver & Barnes, 2010). Therefore, some progressive media scholars have called for moving beyond the stereotypical narratives to provide more equitable coverage of athletes with disabilities (e.g., Hardin & Hardin, 2004). The literature has established conclusively that media descriptions have contributed to the stereotypical views of athletes with disabilities.

The close association between the supercrip narrative and the theme of individualism as it is articulated in U.S. culture is attributable to the fact that U.S. sports media have tended to valorize neoliberal ideals such as rugged individualism and hard work for the purpose of remasculinizing the culture (Kusz, 2007), especially in the wake of 9/ 11 (Falcous & Silk, 2006). The heroic conception of individual athletes reinforces the emphasis on strength, power, and male dominance associated with rugged individualism (Butterworth, 2007). Thus, the supercrip narrative, originating in and perpetuating ableism, emphasizes individual efforts to overcome disabilities and display ablebodiedness. Studies of the news coverage of the Paralympic Games have, naturally, identified the supercrip narrative (Brooke, 2019;McGillivray et al., 2019;Souza & Brittain, 2020). Pullen et al. (2020) argued that "able-nationalism" in sports emphasizes the inclusion of certain disabilities and the othering of individuals with disabilities who do not display exceptional performance.

The supercrip narrative has been the most common of these narratives in the news coverage of athletes with disabilities and the Paralympic Games (Hargreaves & Hardin, 2009;Hodges et al., 2015;Silva & Howe, 2012). Berger (2008) described supercrips as "those individuals whose inspirational stories of courage, dedication, and hard work prove that it can be done, that one can defy the odds and accomplish the impossible" (p. 648).

sections.header_raw: The Paralympic Games as a National Project
sections.header_norm: the paralympic games as a national project
paragraphs: Media outlets also tend to frame their coverage of the Paralympic Games so as to function as a promotional tool of public diplomacy, especially for the host countries. The close association between mega sports events and international politics includes the so-called "ping-pong diplomacy" between the United States and China in the early 1970s, and the female ice hockey team made up of both North and South Korean athletes that competed in the 2018 Pyeongchang Winter Games (Blustein, 1991;Moon et al., 2018). Thus, governments host large-scale sports events, including the Olympic Games, Paralympic Games, and World Cup, in large part to promote national prestige, staging sports as a political spectacle that reproduces national values (Houlihan, 2000).

With their global audiences, mega sports events such as the Paralympic Games offer hosting countries opportunities to cultivate attractive national brands (Fan, 2010) and to showcase national achievements for both domestic and international audiences (Grix & Houlihan, 2014). Cha (2009) noted that hosting countries' governments utilized this representational image. The events often overtly promote universal values such as fair play, worldwide peace, unity, and friendship (Roche, 2002) as well as the host country's economic vitality, culture, and outlook (Ayhan, 2017). The South Korean government, for instance, has promoted athletes with global reputations as a source of national unity with the assistance of national media outlets (Cho, 2008) and provided significant support for the 2018 Winter Paralympic Games in Pyeongchang.

sections.paper_id: Yoo et al. - 2024 - Cross-National News Narratives of the Paralympic Games Computational Text Analysis of the Media Cov
sections.header_raw: Comparison of the Coverage of the Paralympics by U.S. and South Korean Media
sections.header_norm: comparison of the coverage of the paralympics by u s and south korean media
paragraphs: Because enormous numbers of spectators watch and read about mega sports events using various forms of media, the media coverage can enhance the prominence of the participating countries. U.S. media outlets have consistently depicted American athletes as skilled and courageous individuals (Billings & Eastman, 2003). The representations of the Olympic Games in the South Korean media have likewise been influential in shaping the national image as an innovator in internet technology as well as promoting a peaceful relationship between North and South Korea (Ayhan, 2017).

To assess the differences in the media coverage of the Paralympic Games by the U.S. and South Korean media, we compared the two countries' dominant narratives relating to the games with respect to their national and cultural backgrounds, with ableism and the supercrip narrative representing U.S. individualism and government efforts to project positive images of the games overall representing Korean collectivism. As mentioned, previous cross-national comparisons of the news coverage of the Paralympics have focused on the supercrip narrative (Baek, 2012;Schantz & Gilbert, 2001) and marginalized female athletes with disabilities (Buysse & Borcherding, 2010;Pappous et al., 2011). Our analysis of all of the mainstream coverage of the Paralympic Games involved a large number of news articles published over the past two decades through a computational big data analysis. The archives of the major newspapers in each country provided the large amount of text necessary for a cross-national comparison designed to answer our main research question:
RQ: What topics have the coverage of the Paralympic Games by U.S. and South Korean media outlets over the past two decades emphasized?

sections.paper_id: Yoo et al. - 2024 - Cross-National News Narratives of the Paralympic Games Computational Text Analysis of the Media Cov
Yoo et al. - 2024 - Cross-National News Narratives of the Paralympic Games Computational Text Analysis of the Media Cov
paragraphs: We first collected the relevant articles from mainstream newspapers in each country by means of a search with the keyword "Paralympic Games." The time range for the search was January 1, 2000, through December 31, 2020. We chose this time period because each country hosted the Winter Olympic Games, specifically, the 2002 Salt Lake City Games in the United States and the 2018 Pyeongchang Games in South Korea. We used Lexis Nexis to identify news articles published in the United States and the computational crawling method-adapting RStudio, an open-source computer programming software-to gather articles in South Korean newspapers. We collected every news article that included the keyword "Paralympic Games." The sample of articles in U.S. newspapers totaled 1243, of which 683 were from the New York Times, 282 from the Washington Post, and 287 from USA Today. The sample of articles in South Korean newspapers totaled 1924, of which 205 were from Chodsun Ilbo, 203 from Joongang Ilbo, 388 from Donga Ilbo, 644 from Kyunghyang Sinmun, and 366 from Hankyoreh. We chose these newspapers because they were the most-read national newspapers in each country during the period covered by the study (Sun et al., 2020;Yun et al., 2012). We acknowledge that the sample did not represent the full political spectrum in either country. Also, our analysis did not take into account the gender of the athletes.

sections.paper_id: Yoo et al. - 2024 - Cross-National News Narratives of the Paralympic Games Computational Text Analysis of the Media Cov
paragraphs: To interpret the latent topics in the U.S. and South Korean newspaper coverage, we conducted LDA topic modeling, a machine learning technique for identifying thematic structures within a large volume of documents. We followed Günther and Domahidi (2017) in defining a topic loosely as something that is being talked or written about. LDA topic modeling produces a set of topics in the form of groups of keywords that tend to occur in proximity within a corpus of news articles. In this way, researchers can process volumes of text too large to be coded by hand to arrive at a more nuanced interpretation of texts-in this case, newspaper articles-than other computational methods yield (Guo et al., 2016). Also, the method requires no human input to classify data and results in a broader interpretation of texts (Tian & Stewart, 2005).

The LDA topic modeling technique detects the co-occurrence of words in texts, making it an endogenous natural language processing technique for the semantic analysis of a large corpus that involves establishing structural results (Cambria & White, 2014). The topic portion of each document or period is sampled from a family of continuous multivariate probability distributions known as a Dirichlet distribution (Johnson et al., 1997). Each item category in a collection (e.g., each document in a corpus of texts) is modeled as a finite mixture of an underlying set of topics (Gross & Murthy, 2014). After the creation of the specific model, a vector serves to represent the distribution of topics within documents and to calculate the distances among various documents that reflect their similarities so that similar documents show similar distributions over topics.

Prior to the modeling, we conducted preprocessing to remove stopwords and redundant signals from our sample texts. For the modeling, we used the "topicmodels" package in RStudio to identify the topics and keywords. We then reexamined the original news articles containing the keywords that occurred together within the topics, interpreted the meanings of the topics, and categorized them into specific themes. Our in-depth interpretation of the results involved collating the topics into themes using the qualitative analysis approach developed by Shahin and Zheng (2018; see also Shahin, 2020). Finally, we confirmed seven themes after qualitative interpretations of original news articles.

sections.header_raw: Optimal Number of Topics and Results of the LDA Topic Modeling
sections.header_norm: optimal number of topics and results of the lda topic modeling
paragraphs: We used RStudio to conduct topic coherence evaluation to determine the optimal number of topics (Fu et al., 2021) into which to categorize the enormous number of words in the newspaper coverage. We based this determination on the assumption that the words comprising the topic were coherent. The coherence score for the specific number of topics increases with the likelihood that the words within the topics appear together. The number of topics with the highest coherence scores represents the optimum for the topic modeling (O'Callaghan et al., 2015). Based on these coherence scores, we set the maximum number of topics at 10 and selected 10 topics each for the U.S. (Figure 1) and South Korean coverage (Figure 2).

In our collation of the topics into multiple themes, those in the U.S. news coverage (Table 1) included "ableism, supercrip, and rugged individualism" (37%), "Paralympics and public diplomacy" (27%), "multiple issues in the Paralympic Games" (30.5%), and "unidentifiable topics" (5.5%). The themes in the South Korean news coverage (Table 2) included "nationwide effort for the 2018 Winter Paralympic Games" (48.3%), "the supercrip narrative" and "patriotism" (12.5%), and "Paralympics and public diplomacy" (39.2%).

We based this determination on the assumption that the words comprising the topic were coherent. The coherence score for the specific number of topics increases with the likelihood that the words within the topics appear together.

paragraphs: Our analysis identified two topics in the U.S. newspaper coverage related to the description of the Paralympic Games and athletes with disabilities. Topic 1 included the keywords "able-bodied" and "disabled." Also in Topic 1, the keyword "training" represents the supercrip narrative in terms of emphasizing the hard work of athletes with disabilities and comparing their performances with those of "able-bodied" athletes. For example: Those coaches, [Josh] McKinney [a former member of the U.S. Paralympic Soccer Team] said, had the patience to give him extra time to master the skills he needed to be competitive with other, able-bodied athletes. (Irvin, 2002) This weekend, Ottmueller, 17, and Brown, 15, are competing in their state track and field meets in the pole vault, [the two being] pioneers with severe visual impairment who are further redefining who is considered an able-bodied athlete and who is considered a disabled one. (Longman, 2013) He [Dalton Herendeen, a swimmer] continued to adapt with prosthetics, which sometimes rusted quickly because of the amount of time he was spending in the pool. He excelled against able-bodied swimmers in middle school and high school. . . . He was stunned to see how badly he limped with his prosthetic. He has never felt that kind of hitch in the water. (Stubbs, 2016) Several keywords in Topic 2, such as "gold," "medal," and "win," also highlighted athletic achievements. Also, in Topic 2, the keyword "McFadden" refers to Tatyana McFadden, a U.S. track-and-field athlete with disabilities. The media reports praised such achievements as heroic (Schell & Rodriguez, 2001), and Hardin and Hardin (2003) argued that rugged individualism could help athletes with disabilities to overcome them. The analysis identified quotes such as the following that supported this interpretation:

In 2012, even as he [Brad Snyder, a Paralympic swimmer] was trying to make sense of this new, darkened world he had unwittingly entered, he was "deployed" to London, where he came back with two gold medals and one silver. (Sheinin, 2016) Howitt helped the U.S. women's wheelchair basketball team win a gold medal at the 2004 Paralympic Games. . . . "I've just always been really competitive," Howitt said of her love for wheelchair basketball. "It was a sport where I could be that aggressive, competitive person, and it was socially acceptable." (Fern, 2004) After taking to sports in her childhood, Tatyana McFadden fought through the courts in high school in Maryland to compete against able-bodied runners. . . . As McFadden did a set of pull-ups, Bleakney [her track coach] pointed to her impressive back muscles. "That's why we call her the beast," he said. (Strauss, 2013) Some of the keywords in Topic 3, such as "parents," "children," "home," and "family," were associated with the supercrip narrative in the context of personal stories about athletes with disabilities:

"Then I heard about her story and was just dumbfounded by how much she's had to overcome her entire life, but she's the last person to hold a grudge. I take a lot of inspiration from that," Mikaela Shiffrin [a skier] on Oksana Masters [a Paralympic rower and skier] (Parker-Pope, 2018)

"She [Jessica Long, a Paralympic swimmer] was swimming with regular kids," said her mother, Beth, "and was coming in average against them." That caught the attention of some people who work with disabled swimmers. Once Jessica began competing against them, she started setting records almost immediately. Hardin and Hardin's (2003) argument about rugged individualism is based in part on the notion that athletes with disabilities may associate their personal achievements with such keywords as "aggressive" and "competitiveness." Our interpretation of the supercrip narrative is consistent with the argument by Kim et al. (2018) that the media coverage of the Paralympic Games has emphasized personal and emotional stories about individual athletes with disabilities. Matt King, a tandem cyclist, is among those who have spoken of the need to challenge the supercrip stereotype, which, in his case, meant being depicted as a "normal" athlete rather than a blind person (Litsky, 1997). We found that none of the keywords in the topics relating to the U.S. coverage were associated with an effort on the part of the government to support athletes with disabilities. Also, the U.S. news articles, rather than depicting athletes with disabilities as "normal" athletes, compared them with "able-bodied" athletes, thereby highlighting their success in overcoming their disabilities.

sections.header_raw: The Nationwide Effort in Support of the 2018 Winter Paralympic Games
sections.header_norm: the nationwide effort in support of the 2018 winter paralympic games
paragraphs: LDA topic modeling for the South Korean newspaper coverage of the Paralympic Games yielded 10 topics, and among them, five topics indicated a significant amount of interest in the 2018 games when South Korea was the host country. We translated all of the South Korean newspaper articles into English. Topic 1 concerns the educational dimension of the games emphasized by the South Korean government: Sang-gon Kim, the Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Education of South Korea, visited Hoenggye Elementary School in Pyeongchang, Gangwon province, to instill in students the Olympic spirit. . . . As a former professor, Prime Minister Kim explained Olympic ideals such as "excellence, friendship, and respect" and Paralympic ideals such as "bravery, inspiration, determination, and equality" to students. (Kim, 2017) Topic 2 concerns the opening ceremony and exhibitions during the 2018 Olympic and Paralympic Games as part of the effort to promote a positive image of South Korea, as another article from the same newspaper shows:

During the 2018 Winter Olympic and Paralympic Games, multiple exhibitions and concerts will also be launched. . . . The Olympic Games means a festival for the athletes and a time to share in cultural exchange for the purpose of peace and unity. (Choi, 2018) Topic 3 refers to the marketing efforts by the South Korean government's organizing committee as part of the national effort to promote the 2018 games:

The 2018 Winter Olympic Games Organizing Committee launched a sale of commemorative merchandise. . . . Among this merchandise, "Bandabi," the Winter Olympic Games mascot, was prominent. (Kim, 2018) Topic 4 concerns the South Korean government's efforts to encourage tourism and show the prowess of South Korean industry. The terms "hydrogen" and "automotive" are associated with a sales promotion by Hyundai Motors during the 2018 Olympic and Paralympic Games. Other terms in this topic included language associated with the government's promotion of national economic interests, including Pyeongchang as a tourist destination:

To host the 2018 Winter Olympic Games and encourage more sightseeing, the Korean Tourism Organization announced "the month of Pyeongchang Sightseeing." A wide range of events will be offered to visitors during their travels. (Yang, 2018) Topic 5 includes discussion of the South Korean government's efforts to include North Korea in the 2018 games, especially putting together the national ice hockey team consisting of both North and South Korean athletes mentioned earlier: President Moon emphasized the role of a joint national team as a stepping-stone to build a friendly relationship between North and South Koreas. (Moon et al., 2018) The promotion of the national image and values in the coverage of the Paralympic Games by the national South Korean news media is evident with respect to cultural events, merchandizing, and cutting-edge technologies and tourism (Topics 2, 3, and 4, respectively). The coverage likewise characterized the government's efforts as exploiting the educational value of the 2018 games (Topic 1) and the unified ice hockey team as a signal of the desire for reconciliation with the North (Topic 5). However, the Paralympic Games and the athletes with disabilities who competed in them were not the main focus, like Kell et al. (2008) who argued that the Paralympics were treated as "nothing more than a sideshow to the Olympic Games" (p. 65). Notably, in these five topics, the South Korean coverage contained none of the specific terms describing the athletic skills and performances that we found in the U.S. coverage.

sections.header_raw: The Supercrip Narrative and Patriotism in the South Korean Coverage
sections.header_norm: the supercrip narrative and patriotism in the south korean coverage
paragraphs: One theme in the South Korean coverage was the supercrip narrative (Topic 6). This narrative was associated with stories about a former South Korean army sergeant named Jae-Heon Ha, who lost his legs to a landmine while serving in the Korean Demilitarized Zone. Following his discharge because of his disability, he earned distinction as a rowing athlete. The words "wheelchair," "disability," "injury," "leg," "athletes," "sports," "exercise," and "practice" helped to identify articles that discuss athletes with disabilities, while keywords such as "gold medal" may indicate their achievements. Similarly, the keyword "confidence" may connote the determination of athletes with disabilities to overcome those disabilities:

After three years and five months, sergeant Jae-Heon Ha will be discharged from the South Korean Army. . . . Ha said, "As a rowing athlete, I will practice hard, attend the 2020 Tokyo Paralympic Games, and earn a gold medal." Ha also said, "I hope to show the confidence, known as 'can-do' spirit, to people who have supported me." (Hong, 2019) The story of a former soldier training for the games-in effect, overcoming his disability for his country-is inspirational. However, in like manner as Singaporean (Brooke, 2019) and U.K. (Pullen & Silk, 2020) media that linked the Paralympics to patriotic sentiment by representing athletes with disabilities as national heroes, the stories about Ha linked his supercrip narrative with patriotism by describing him as a "hero" and mentioning his aim of earning a gold medal at the 2024 Paralympics. Also, like the supercrip narrative, Ha's story distracted attention from the other athletes with disabilities at the games (Kim et al., 2018).

paragraphs: International relationships were a common topic in the coverage of the Paralympic Games by both South Korean and U.S. media outlets. Thus, 2 of the 10 topics identified in the U.S. news coverage pertained to public diplomacy, specifically, the relationship between the United States and East Asian countries, including North Korea (Topic 4), and the tension between the United States and Russia (Topic 5). These topics appeared 2 months before the 2018 games and during the 2014 Winter Olympics held in Russia, respectively.

In the South Korean news coverage, 4 of the 10 topics pertained to public diplomacy, specifically, the relationship between North and South Korea (Topics 7, 8, and 9) and relations with Japan (Topic 10). The main theme of these articles was the contentious nature of public diplomacy. For South Korea, the host country for the 2018 Winter Olympics and Paralympics, such mega sports events serve as tools of public diplomacy (Grix, 2012). Also, while some of the keywords associated with the relationship with Japan (Topic 10) referred to the postponement of the 2020 Summer Olympics in Tokyo because of the COVID-19 pandemic, the theme of conflict surfaced regarding the alleged incorporation of the colonial-era rising sun symbol into the design of a medal for the associated 2020 Paralympics.

Other Paralympic Games in the U.S. Coverage Much of the U.S. news coverage presented the stories of individual competitors in the Paralympic Games over the past 20 years (Topics 6-10). Thus, the frequent topics included Japan's bid to host the 2020 Olympics and Paralympics amid the pandemic (Topic 6) and Oscar Pistorius, a South African sprinter tried for murdering his girlfriend (Topic 7), whose story was told in articles in which keywords such as "legs," "meter," and "compete" as well other keywords relating to the criminal proceedings. The U.S. coverage also touched on the 2012 Olympic and Paralympic Games in London (Topic 8) and the 2008 games in Beijing (Topic 9), though without any specific language distinguishing the Paralympics or athletes with disabilities from the other athletes. The topic of general sports activities (Topic 10) included no keywords suggesting individualistic culture or ableism-or collectivism.

sections.paper_id: Yoo et al. - 2024 - Cross-National News Narratives of the Paralympic Games Computational Text Analysis of the Media Cov
paragraphs: Using computational big data, specifically, LDA topic modeling, we analyzed articles in major national newspapers in both countries mentioning the games over the past two decades and inferred the association between national culture and the dominant topics. We found in this study that the focus of the coverage of the Paralympic Games by U.S. and South Korean news outlets reflected the distinct cultures of those countries. This research thus makes two significant contributions to the study of the media discourse about the Paralympics. First, the results indicated that the coverage by the media in the two countries shared some themes, such as the importance of the games as a tool of public diplomacy relating to the relationship between North and South Korea. On the other hand, the distinctive cultural backgrounds of the United States and South Koreawhich we followed previous scholars in associating with rugged individualism and collectivism, respectively-shaped the dominant narratives. Thus, the topics covered by the U.S. media outlets showed more of a preference for stories about the athletes with disabilities, while the topics favored in South Korean coverage dealt with efforts by the government to promote mega sports events. Specifically, the U.S. coverage often featured various forms of the "supercrip narrative," which emphasizes the hard work done by athletes with disabilities and compares their athletic achievements with those of "able-bodied" athletes. As previous studies (Hardin & Hardin, 2003;Kim et al., 2018;Litsky, 1997) have indicated, rugged individualist discourse, when applied to athletes with disabilities, conveys the notion that society as a whole has relatively little responsibility to accommodate those with disabilities, but rather that it is up to individuals to overcome them. Our results corroborate two decades of media scholarship on the supercrip narrative and help to focus critical attention on journalists' stereotyping of athletes with disabilities.

The South Korean coverage, by contrast, focused on the nationwide effort by the government to promote the 2018 Winter Olympic and Paralympic Games. This finding is consistent with previous findings regarding the collectivist nature of South Korean society (Hofstede, 2001;Lee, 2001). Such coverage highlights national unity and the public benefits of the games for national development. Among a total of 1924 Korean news articles over 20 years, more than half (56.3%) were published between February 1, 2018, one week before the 2018 Winter Olympic and Paralympic Games (n = 1084) and 2020. These percentages and numbers reflect the national interest in hosting mega sports events, but, even so, the coverage of the athletes with disabilities themselves was fairly scant. Even the news stories about Jae-Heon Ha in the South Korean news outlets describing him as a heroic disabled former soldier competing for South Korea focused on patriotic sentiment rather than his individual achievement (Brooke, 2019).

In sum, our cross-national comparison identified subtle differences between the U.S. and South Korean coverage of the Paralympics. In this respect, it provides a template for cross-national comparisons of the coverage of sporting events. Further, we have expanded the range of methodologies for studying these issues by adapting LDA topic modeling using a large volume of words and texts to reveal patterns in media coverage to analyze long periods of news coverage of specific topics by multiple outlets in multiple countries quickly and efficiently, since this type of modeling provides diverse language packages. Such large-scale analysis of texts (Tian & Stewart, 2005) can be applied to other countries and events.

Our analysis did not determine whether a change has occurred in the terminology used to describe athletes with disabilities and para-sports. Blauwet and Willick (2012) pointed out that the media coverage has increased awareness of the Paralympics and fostered the use of inclusive terms for athletes with disabilities to promote disability rights and social integration. Future studies could make use of computational analysis to further elucidate this trend. The present study provides insight into the distinct perspectives of U.S. and Korean culture that manifest in media representations of the Paralympic Games. The findings presented here also can inform efforts to draft guidelines for media outlets in these and other countries that call for describing athletes with disabilities in the same terms as able-bodied athletes.
